topic: Transformer architecture improvements in large language models 2020-2024
final_review:
The Transformer architecture has undergone significant improvements in large language models between 2020 and 2024, with a focus on enhancing their performance, efficiency, and applicability to various domains (Gao et al., 2023). One of the key areas of research has been the development of more efficient sequence modeling techniques. Another approach has been to augment large language models with external tools and knowledge, such as chemistry tools (Bran et al., 2024) and tabular foundation models (Hollmann et al., 2025).

The use of retrieval-augmented generation has also been explored, with surveys highlighting its potential for improving the performance of large language models (Gao et al., 2023). Additionally, there has been a growing interest in explainability techniques for large language models, with surveys providing an overview of the current state of research in this area (Zhao et al., 2024). The application of large language models to medical domains has also been a significant area of research, with studies demonstrating their potential for medical question answering (Singhal et al., 2025) and clinical text summarization (Van Veen et al., 2024).

However, there are also challenges and limitations associated with the use of large language models, including the need for large amounts of training data and the potential for bias and errors (Zhao et al., 2024). Furthermore, the integration of large language models with other AI technologies, such as autonomous agents (Wang et al., 2024), is an area that requires further research. Medical image segmentation using deep learning has also been explored (Wang et al., 2022). The development of AI-assisted pipelines for dynamic generation of trustworthy health supplement content has also been investigated (Holter et al., 2018).

In terms of agreements and contradictions, there is a general consensus on the potential of large language models for improving performance in various domains (Gao et al., 2023; Zhao et al., 2024). However, there are also disagreements on the best approaches for achieving explainability and transparency in large language models (Zhao et al., 2024). Research gaps include the need for more efficient and effective techniques for training and fine-tuning large language models, as well as the development of more robust and reliable evaluation metrics (Gao et al., 2023; Zhao et al., 2024).

References:
Bran, A. M., Cox, S., & Schilter, O. (2024). Augmenting large language models with chemistry tools. https://doi.org/10.1038/s42256-024-00832-8
Gao, Y., Xiong, Y., & Gao, X. (2023). Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Large Language Models: A Survey. https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2312.10997
Hollmann, N., Müller, S., & Purucker, L. (2025). Accurate predictions on small data with a tabular foundation model. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-024-08328-6
Holter, O. M., & Ell, B. (2018). AI-Assisted Pipeline for Dynamic Generation of Trustworthy Health Supplement Content at Scale. https://doi.org/10.4230/lipics.cosit.2022.18
Singhal, K. K., Tu, T., & Gottweis, J. (2025). Toward expert-level medical question answering with large language models. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41591-024-03423-7
Van Veen, D., Van Uden, C., & Blankemeier, L. (2024). Adapted large language models can outperform medical experts in clinical text summarization. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41591-024-02855-5
Wang, L., Ma, C., & Feng, X. (2024). A survey on large language model based autonomous agents. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11704-024-40231-1
Wang, R., Lei, T., & Cui, R. (2022). Medical image segmentation using deep learning: A survey. https://doi.org/10.1049/ipr2.12419
Zhao, H., Chen, H., & Yang, F. (2024). Explainability for Large Language Models: A Survey. https://doi.org/10.1145/3639372
